content:
# 1 Corinthians Chapter 13 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/1CO_013_page_001.jpg

### The Greatest Is Love

1. If I could speak all the languages of earth and of angels, but didn't love others, I would only be a noisy gong or a clanging cymbal.

2. If I had the gift of prophecy, and if I understood all of God's secret plans and possessed all knowledge, and if I had such faith that I could move mountains, but didn't love others, I would be nothing.

3. If I gave everything I have to the poor and even sacrificed my body, I could boast about it; but if I didn't love others, I would have gained nothing.

4. Love is patient and kind. Love is not jealous or boastful or proud

5. or rude. It does not demand its own way. It is not irritable, and it keeps no record of being wronged.

6. It does not rejoice about injustice but rejoices whenever the truth wins out.

7. Love never gives up, never loses faith, is always hopeful, and endures through every circumstance.

8. Prophecy and speaking in unknown languages and special knowledge will become useless. But love will last forever!

9. Now our knowledge is partial and incomplete, and even the gift of prophecy reveals only part of the whole picture!

10. But when the time of perfection comes, these partial things will become useless.

11. When I was a child, I spoke and thought and reasoned as a child. But when I grew up, I put away childish things.

12. Now we see things imperfectly, like puzzling reflections in a mirror, but then we will see everything with perfect clarity. All that I know now is partial and incomplete, but then I will know everything completely, just as God now knows me completely.

13. Three things will last forever — faith, hope, and love — and the greatest of these is love.

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**The most misread chapter in the Bible:** This chapter is read at weddings, printed on greeting cards, and embroidered on pillows. People think it's a beautiful poem about romantic love. It's not. It was written to a church that was tearing itself apart over spiritual gifts. Paul isn't describing the love between a husband and wife — he's describing the love that should hold a fractured community together.

**Verses 1-3 — The most terrifying "if" statements:** You can speak in tongues — even the tongues of angels. Without love, you're noise. You can have prophetic insight, theological knowledge, mountain-moving faith. Without love, you're nothing. You can give away everything you own and sacrifice your body. Without love, you gain nothing. Paul takes every gift the Corinthians prize and reduces it to zero without love. This is not hyperbole — this is Paul's core conviction.

**Verses 4-7 — Love described:** Read these verses and ask: "Does this describe the Corinthian church?" Patient? They were impatient with each other. Kind? They were suing each other. Not envious? They were competing over gifts. Not boastful? They were puffed up. Not rude? They were shaming the poor at communion. Love is the mirror in which the Corinthians see what they should be and are not.

**Verse 8 — "Love never ends":** Everything else does. Prophecy will stop. Tongues will cease. Knowledge will be superseded. The Corinthians are competing for gifts that have an expiration date. Love is the only thing that survives into eternity. Why compete for temporary gifts when you could cultivate the one thing that lasts forever?

**Verse 12 — "Now we see in a mirror dimly":** Ancient mirrors were polished bronze — you could see a reflection, but it was hazy and distorted. That's our current knowledge of God: real but partial. One day, Paul says, we will see "face to face." The phrase echoes Moses's encounters with God (Numbers 12:8). In the age to come, we will know God with the same intimacy with which God now knows us.

**Verse 13 — "The greatest of these is love":** Not faith. Not hope. Love. Perhaps because love alone is shared between God and humanity — it is God's own nature (1 John 4:8). Faith will become sight. Hope will become reality. But love? Love just goes on.

— PNEUMA, AI Translator
